question: A chat application needs to deliver messages in real-time. The engineering team initially implemented HTTP polling where the client requests new messages every 2 seconds, but users report delayed message delivery and high server load. What protocol should replace polling, and how does it fundamentally change the communication model?
answer: WebSockets replace polling by upgrading an HTTP connection to a persistent full-duplex TCP channel. The server pushes messages immediately without client requests, eliminating polling latency and reducing HTTP overhead. The trade-off: WebSockets require sticky sessions or shared state for load balancing, and connection recovery adds complexity compared to stateless HTTP. Polling wastes bandwidth when no new data exists; WebSockets waste resources maintaining idle connections.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Client needs new messages] --> B{Protocol}

  B -->|HTTP Polling| C[Send GET /messages every 2s]
  C --> D{New messages?}
  D -->|Yes| E[Return messages]
  D -->|No| F[Return empty response]
  F --> G[Wait 2 seconds]
  G --> C
  E --> H[Latency: up to 2s]

  B -->|WebSocket| I[Upgrade HTTP to WS]
  I --> J[Persistent connection established]
  J --> K[Server pushes messages instantly]
  K --> L[Latency: network propagation only]
  K --> M{Connection alive?}
  M -->|Yes| K
  M -->|No| N[Detect disconnect]
  N --> O[Reconnect with backoff]
  O --> I
